Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Passing tests: 15 (verified green)
Recent Green phase: Activated "cursed sword + plain amulet → 231" multi-item modifier-scope test; passed with no code change (item surcharges are per-item, policy modifiers on policy base — already correct).

Refactor while keeping all tests green.

PROCEDURE: Before reading test results, run `rm -rf node_modules/.vitest node_modules/.vite` then `pnpm test`. Confirm "15 passed | 25 todo" before returning.

ABSOLUTELY CRITICAL: src/claim-office.ts and src/claim-office.spec.ts must contain ONLY valid TypeScript. No prose/notes/summary in either source file — report ONLY in your returned message.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

<tool_use_error>String to replace not found in file.
String: export function claimOffice(request: ClaimRequest): number {
  const { policyBase, modifier, items = [] } = request;

  let policyAmount = policyBase;
  if (modifier === "haunted") {
    policyAmount = policyBase * 1.5;
  } else if (modifier === "blessed") {
    policyAmount = policyBase * 0.8;
  }

  let itemsTotal = 0;
  for (const item of items) {
    let itemAmount = item.base;
    if (item.kind === "cursed") {
      itemAmount = item.base + 30;
    }
    itemsTotal = itemsTotal + itemAmount;
  }

  return policyAmount + itemsTotal;
}</tool_use_error>

content.text:
My initial assumptions about the code were wrong — the actual implementation is quite different and already well-factored. My Edit failed (correctly, since that code doesn't exist), so the file is untouched. Tests confirm 15 passed | 25 todo. Let me now do a proper evaluation of the real code.

The actual `src/claim-office.ts` is already in excellent shape. Let me evaluate it against the Four Rules.

**Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)**

The functions are all small, single-purpose, and well-named: `unitPriceFor`, `isRune`, `isMainItem`, `runeBasePremium`, `sumOver`, `sumUnitPrices`, `countRunes`, `totalBasePremium`, `isHighlyEnchanted`, `itemSurchargeRate`, `itemSurcharges`, `totalSurcharges`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `isLoyal`, `loyaltyDiscount`, `quote`. Names clearly reveal intent. No naming improvement needed.

**Step 2: Initial APP Mass (the `quote` function, the most recently touched code)**

```
quote: invocations totalBasePremium, totalSurcharges, firstInsuranceSurcharge,
       loyaltyDiscount, Math.ceil (5×2=10); bindings policyBase, input, premiumBeforeFee (3);
       arithmetic +,+,- (3×2=6) → roughly 19
```

**Step 3: Apply Four Rules**

- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** ✅ 15 passing.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** Already strong. One small opportunity: in `quote`, the variable `premiumBeforeFee` is computed then `PROCESSING_FEE` is added inside `Math.ceil`. The name `premiumBeforeFee` is accurate. No clear win.
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication):** The `firstInsuranceSurcharge`/`loyaltyDiscount` both take `policyBase`; `sumOver` already abstracts the reduce duplication. No remaining duplication.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** The code is decomposed into many tiny helpers. This is intentional clarity, not over-engineering — each maps to a named domain concept. Collapsing them would hurt Rule 2.

Let me look for the one genuinely defensible micro-improvement: `isMainItem` is defined as `!isRune(item)` and used once. Inlining would reduce an element (Rule 4) but `isMainItem` reads better at the call site than `(item) => !isRune(item)`. Keeping it serves Rule 2, which trumps Rule 4.

Let me check the `claim` stub more carefully — it's a placeholder with `(...args: unknown[]): unknown`. This is the one element that is genuinely vestigial scaffolding, but removing it would break the spec import (`import { quote, claim }`). It must stay.

After careful review, the code is already at a local optimum for the Four Rules. Per the mandatory-attempt requirement, I evaluated one candidate refactoring (inlining the single-use `isMainItem`) and rejected it because it trades a Rule 2 win (intent-revealing call site) for a marginal Rule 4 gain — clarity trumps fewest-elements.

Let me run the final confirmation.

content.text:
Confirmed: **15 passed | 25 todo**. The source files contain only valid TypeScript (no changes were made — my one Edit attempt failed against a wrong assumption and left the file untouched).

## Refactoring Report

**Outcome: No code change applied — current implementation evaluated as already optimal.**

The actual `src/claim-office.ts` (a quote/claim insurance domain) is already cleanly decomposed into small, single-purpose, intent-revealing functions. I evaluated it fully against the Four Rules of Simple Design.

**Step 1 — Naming (first priority):** All identifiers clearly reveal intent: `unitPriceFor`, `runeBasePremium`, `isHighlyEnchanted`, `itemSurchargeRate`, `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `loyaltyDiscount`, `totalBasePremium`, `sumOver`, etc. No rename improves clarity.

**Step 2 — APP mass (the `quote` function, last touched in Green):** ~19 (5 invocations ×2, 3 bindings, 3 arithmetic ops ×2). Already minimal for the policy formula it expresses; the surrounding work is delegated to named helpers rather than inlined.

**Step 3 — Four Rules:**
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): 15/15 green, before and after.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Strong — each helper maps to one named domain concept.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): The repeated `items.reduce(...)` pattern is already abstracted behind `sumOver`; no remaining duplication.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): The many small helpers are intentional clarity, not over-engineering.

**Mandatory candidate considered and rejected:** Inlining the single-use `isMainItem` (= `!isRune`) would shave one element (Rule 4 gain) but worsen the `totalBasePremium` call site readability. Rule 2 trumps Rule 4, so I kept it.

**Mass change:** none (no change applied). **Tests: all passing (15 passed | 25 todo).**

- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_22-17-53_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.2-with-why-cleaned_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
